prompt:
We just finished a 2-week A/B test on our main lead gen landing page. Here are the results:

Variant A (Control — current page):
  Visitors: 1,247
  Form submissions: 112
  Conversion rate: 8.98%
  Avg time on page: 1:42
  Bounce rate: 61%
  Form field count: 7 (name, email, company, title, phone, company size, use case)
  Post-submission MQL rate: 34%

Variant B (Challenger — new design):
  Visitors: 1,253
  Form submissions: 198
  Conversion rate: 15.80%
  Avg time on page: 2:18
  Bounce rate: 44%
  Form field count: 4 (name, work email, company, title)
  Post-submission MQL rate: 19%

So Variant B gets way more conversions but the MQL rate dropped hard. The team is split on which to ship. We need 80 MQLs/month from this page to hit target.

Current monthly traffic to the page: ~2,600 visitors.

What's your analysis?
expectations: To reframe the team's decision around pipeline impact rather than conversion vanity metrics, effective MQL yield per visitor is calculated for both variants (A: 8.98% x 34% = 3.05% = ~79 MQLs/month; B: 15.80% x 19% = 3.00% = ~78 MQLs/month) — exposing that the raw conversion lift produces zero net gain in qualified pipeline

To tell the CMO whether this page can hit the 80 MQL/month target, both variants are assessed against the target and both are found insufficient — the analysis frames this as 'neither variant solves the business problem' rather than picking a winner from two losing options

To diagnose why the MQL rate dropped so the team can fix it, root cause analysis identifies that removing company size and use case fields eliminated the qualification signal — the form reduction increased volume but removed the data that separates qualified from unqualified leads

To generate a recommendation that actually hits the 80 MQL target, the analysis proposes a specific Variant C: Variant B's 4-field form with progressive profiling on the thank-you page or backend enrichment via Clearbit/ZoomInfo to recover qualification data without adding form friction — with projected MQL yield math

To benchmark both variants against industry standards, conversion rates are compared to the skill's landing page benchmark (average 10-20%) — Variant A (8.98%) classified as below average, Variant B (15.80%) classified as average-to-good — contextualizing the UX improvement as real even though MQL yield is flat

To protect the UX gains worth preserving regardless of which variant ships, the analysis identifies Variant B's lower bounce rate (44% vs 61%) and higher time-on-page (2:18 vs 1:42) as engagement improvements that indicate better page design — these should carry forward into any Variant C
